quickCheck: Am I solving the problem, or just solving the discomfort of not yet moving?
analogyClaim: This is like yanking on every control in turbulence because stillness feels too much like helplessness.
analogyResponse: Visible motion can soothe anxiety without improving the outcome. Good intervention has to beat thoughtful restraint, not merely look busier than it.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label whenever someone acts quickly. Sometimes speed is exactly right. The issue is that action itself is being treated as evidence of quality.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when doing something starts to feel inherently superior to waiting, diagnosing, or preserving optionality.

teachingGauges.label: Common in live pressure
teachingGauges.note: Strong where visible leadership and time pressure are fused.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often clearer to observers than to the actor who feels virtuous urgency.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Motion is easy to confuse with responsibility.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: Becomes vivid as soon as waiting is treated as a live option rather than a failure.

practiceLab.intro: Action bias is attractive because agency feels morally and socially superior to visible restraint.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A problem appears under uncertainty, stress, or observation.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: Quick intervention feels like competence while waiting feels like negligence.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Action becomes the default standard instead of one option that has to earn its place.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Write the best case for watchful inaction or delayed action before committing to visible movement.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: What would I do here if nobody could see whether I acted immediately?

caseStudies.title: Goalkeeper penalty-kick studies
caseStudies.summary: Goalkeepers often dive on penalty kicks even though staying centered can sometimes be statistically better, because visible action feels more defensible than stillness.
caseStudies.whyItFits: The action is partly chosen because inaction feels harder to justify socially if the outcome goes badly.

quickCheck: Which easier question am I answering instead of the one I was actually asked?
analogyClaim: This is like grading a book's argument by how elegant the cover feels in your hands.
analogyResponse: The easier cue may correlate sometimes, but correlation is not permission to forget the original question.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label every time a proxy is used. Proxies can be useful. The problem begins when the proxy quietly replaces the target without acknowledgment.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when a hard attribute such as risk, competence, or probability is being answered through a simpler cue like fear, polish, or familiarity.

teachingGauges.label: Common in fast judgment
teachingGauges.note: This is one of the hidden engines under many other biases.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often only visible after the original question is restated cleanly.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: The substitute answer often feels like the original answer.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: Powerful once learned, but more abstract than many surface-level bias labels.

practiceLab.intro: Attribute substitution is a hidden-question swap. The final answer still sounds responsive, but the underlying judgment target has shifted.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A complex judgment demands effort or unfamiliar calculation.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: A simpler cue offers a fast and confident-seeming answer.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: The easier cue begins masquerading as direct access to the harder attribute.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: State both the target attribute and the tempting proxy in separate sentences before deciding which one is actually being measured.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: What evidence would answer the original hard question directly rather than through a shortcut?

caseStudies.title: Risk judged by affect
caseStudies.summary: People often answer how risky something is by answering how good or bad it feels, letting affect stand in for analytic risk judgment.
caseStudies.whyItFits: An easier evaluative question replaces the harder target attribute while still feeling like the same judgment.

quickCheck: If the system output were hidden, what would my own first pass actually say?
analogyClaim: This is like treating the autopilot as if it were the weather.
analogyResponse: Automation can inform the route without becoming the sky itself. A tool is still one part of the evidential picture, not the whole landscape.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label whenever software is trusted. Some systems deserve significant trust. The issue is overdependence that suppresses correction or independent judgment.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when automated recommendations or alerts are getting more deference than their actual reliability and fit justify.

teachingGauges.label: Common in tool-heavy work
teachingGauges.note: More important as algorithmic decision support spreads.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often visible after an obvious override opportunity was passed up.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Machine output feels like a low-blame source of certainty.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: Requires teaching both tool value and tool limits at the same time.

practiceLab.intro: Automation bias turns recommendation into gravitational center. The system no longer merely informs the judgment; it narrows what judgment feels permissible.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: An automated output arrives before or during human evaluation.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: The output feels objective and safer to follow than a personally owned override.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Human correction weakens, even where contextual evidence points the other way.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Force one independent pass and one explicit review of the system's likely failure conditions.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: What domain assumption is the system making here that I may be forgetting to test?

caseStudies.title: Clinical and cockpit automation examples
caseStudies.summary: Research on automation bias shows that people may miss errors of omission or commission because the system recommendation becomes the assumed baseline.
caseStudies.whyItFits: The automation does not just assist. It begins shaping what the human treats as sufficiently checked.

quickCheck: Would this still seem this plausible if it had not been repeated so much in public?
analogyClaim: This is like mistaking the echo in a canyon for evidence that more people have joined the conversation.
analogyResponse: Louder circulation can create the feel of corroboration even when the same weak claim is only bouncing around the same surface.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label for every popular claim. Sometimes repetition follows strong evidence. The issue is that repetition itself is providing the credibility lift.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when public circulation and social uptake are amplifying plausibility faster than direct evidence is.

teachingGauges.label: Common in media ecosystems
teachingGauges.note: Especially common when speed and virality outrun verification.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Usually easier to detect after the source chain is reconstructed.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Public repetition naturally feels like social confirmation.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: A good entry point for misinformation literacy.

practiceLab.intro: Availability cascade is a social plausibility machine. Repetition and circulation create the impression that many minds and many facts have separately endorsed the same claim.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A claim is vivid enough to circulate and simple enough to repeat.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: The growing familiarity of the claim starts feeling like growing evidence for the claim.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Public visibility and evidential support become harder to distinguish.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Separate source count, repetition count, and actual evidential count before deciding how much weight the claim deserves.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: How many genuinely independent sources support this apart from social repetition?

caseStudies.title: Public-risk scares amplified by repetition
caseStudies.summary: Availability cascades describe how repeated public claims can gain plausibility and policy traction mainly through visibility and reinforcement.
caseStudies.whyItFits: The claim's social uptake becomes part of why it feels increasingly true.

quickCheck: If I did not know how many others endorsed this, would my judgment move?
analogyClaim: This is like choosing a restaurant because the line itself starts tasting like evidence.
analogyResponse: Crowds can contain information, but the crowd is not the meal. Good judgment still asks what the line is a line for.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label whenever social proof matters. Sometimes popularity really does signal quality. The issue is when uptake outruns independent scrutiny.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when popularity itself is doing too much of the persuasive work.

teachingGauges.label: Common in social uptake
teachingGauges.note: Highly visible in markets, politics, and fast sentiment environments.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often visible once adoption numbers are hidden from evaluators.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Popularity feels like a practical shortcut when independent review is costly.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: Very teachable with ordinary consumer and opinion examples.

practiceLab.intro: Bandwagon effect turns visible adoption into an argument. A growing crowd makes independent evaluation feel less necessary and less urgent.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A belief, product, or stance becomes visibly popular.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: Convergence begins to feel like proof that the option has already survived scrutiny.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Adoption momentum starts replacing direct inspection.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Evaluate the reasons once with the popularity signal removed and again with it visible, then compare the difference.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: What part of my preference is coming from the option itself rather than from the reassurance of not standing apart?

caseStudies.title: Electoral and consumer momentum examples
caseStudies.summary: Bandwagon effects are often discussed where visible growth in support creates more support simply because the growth is visible.
caseStudies.whyItFits: The popularity signal itself becomes one of the main causal drivers of later preference.

quickCheck: What distortion do I think only the other side is susceptible to here?
analogyClaim: This is like carrying a smudged lens cleaner and using it only on everyone else's glasses.
analogyResponse: The ability to diagnose distortion in others does not guarantee cleaner vision in yourself. Sometimes it just makes self-exemption easier to narrate.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label as a clever way of saying everyone is equally biased in every case. The issue is asymmetry of self-diagnosis, not flat moral equivalence.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when bias awareness seems to travel outward much more easily than inward.

teachingGauges.label: Common in bias discourse
teachingGauges.note: Paradoxically strong among people who know many bias labels already.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Hard to detect because self-neutrality is built into the experience.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: One's own view rarely feels like one view among many from inside.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: A meta-bias that can survive shallow literacy about bias.

practiceLab.intro: Bias blind spot is what keeps bias education humbling. The more articulate a person is about others' distortions, the easier self-exemption can become.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A conflict, debate, or judgment invites psychological explanation.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: Other people look like interpreters while the self still looks like the witness.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Debiasing becomes a tool for diagnosing rivals instead of disciplining one's own process.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: State the strongest bias risk on your own side before naming it on the opposing side.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: Which bias label gives me the strongest feeling of superiority when I apply it to others?

caseStudies.title: People rate themselves as less biased than others
caseStudies.summary: Research on bias blind spot finds that people often judge themselves as less susceptible to bias than other people even while endorsing the general reality of bias.
caseStudies.whyItFits: The concept is accepted in the abstract but self-application lags far behind other-application.

quickCheck: How much of my memory of this choice is now defending the fact that it was my choice?
analogyClaim: This is like editing the travel brochure after you already bought the ticket.
analogyResponse: The destination may still be worthwhile, but post-choice memory often starts making the purchase look cleaner than the original tradeoff really was.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label whenever someone remains satisfied with a decision. The issue is memory drift and post-choice revaluation, not mere commitment.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when chosen options are remembered as having been stronger and unchosen ones as weaker than the original comparison justified.

teachingGauges.label: Common after commitment
teachingGauges.note: Shows up wherever choices become tied to identity or public commitment.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often visible when old notes are compared to later retellings.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Memory naturally wants coherence after commitment.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: Very teachable through pre-commitment note-taking and later comparison.

practiceLab.intro: Choice-supportive bias is one way the mind turns a messy decision into a cleaner autobiographical story.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A choice has been made and alternatives are now closed or socially costly to revisit.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: The chosen option begins to feel more obviously right than it did at decision time.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Memory edits reduce ambivalence and flatter the chosen path.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Compare the live memory to a preserved record of the original tradeoffs before letting the later story become history.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: What did the rejected option genuinely have going for it at the time I chose against it?

caseStudies.title: Post-choice reevaluation studies
caseStudies.summary: People often remember selected options as better than they originally judged them and rejected options as worse after the fact.
caseStudies.whyItFits: Commitment invites memory repair in favor of the chosen path.

quickCheck: Am I calling the narrower story more probable just because it feels more representative?
analogyClaim: This is like thinking a decorated branch is bigger than the tree because it is more interesting to look at.
analogyResponse: Interesting stories can fit well and still belong inside a larger category that remains more probable. Specificity is not probability.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label every time someone tells a detailed story. The issue is the mathematical mistake of rating the specific conjunction above the broader set that contains it.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when narrative fit or representativeness makes the richer option feel more likely than the more general one.

teachingGauges.label: Common in probability talk
teachingGauges.note: Especially active when stories are vivid and categories are abstract.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Clear once the set relation is named explicitly.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Representativeness feels like a strong cue if the math is not foregrounded.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: One of the best biases for teaching the distance between coherence and probability.

practiceLab.intro: Conjunction fallacy is what happens when narrative fit outruns set logic.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A specific story feels highly representative of a person or event.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: The richer description seems more likely because it seems more revealing.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Specificity gets mistaken for probability, and the narrower event outranks the broader container.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Ask which option contains the other, then remember that the container cannot be less probable than one of its subsets.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: Which option is the superset here, and why am I letting the more vivid subset feel stronger?

caseStudies.title: The Linda problem
caseStudies.summary: In the famous Linda problem, many people judge a more specific description of Linda as more probable than the broader one that logically contains it.
caseStudies.whyItFits: Representativeness and vivid fit defeat the simpler probability rule.

quickCheck: What part of the old false story is still doing reasoning work because the correction never replaced it?
analogyClaim: This is like removing a broken beam from a bridge diagram without drawing a new support line, then wondering why everyone still imagines the old structure.
analogyResponse: Retractions often subtract. Good corrections also rebuild. Otherwise the first explanatory frame keeps living in the empty space.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label whenever someone remembers the original rumor. The key issue is that corrected misinformation still shapes explanation and inference.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when people acknowledge the correction but continue reasoning as if part of the first false account remained true.

teachingGauges.label: Common in misinformation
teachingGauges.note: Especially important where corrections are late, thin, or non-explanatory.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often only visible when the person's later inferences are examined closely.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: People can sincerely accept a correction and still reason with the old story.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: A powerful media-literacy concept because it explains why debunking can feel incomplete.

practiceLab.intro: Continued influence effect is the residue problem of misinformation. A correction can remove a claim without removing the explanatory work the claim was doing.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A vivid misinformation frame arrives early and fills an explanatory gap.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: The original account keeps a cognitive advantage because it remains narratively useful.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Later reasoning still leans on the old frame even after the person endorses the correction.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Replace the false claim with a satisfying alternative explanation instead of offering retraction alone.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: What explanation should occupy the slot the misinformation used to fill?

caseStudies.title: Corrected fire-warehouse narratives
caseStudies.summary: People can continue citing corrected details from a fire story in later inferences when the correction does not provide a strong replacement explanation.
caseStudies.whyItFits: The mind keeps using the first causal frame because the retraction alone did not rebuild the story.

quickCheck: Would my preference stay put if the dominated third option disappeared?
analogyClaim: This is like preferring one painting over another because a third, clearly weaker painting was hung next to only one of them.
analogyResponse: The third painting changed the contrast, not the intrinsic quality of the first two. Context can build preference without improving the option.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label every time an extra option appears. The issue is asymmetric dominance that changes preference between the original options.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when a worse third option is steering preference toward a target option by making it look comparatively stronger.

teachingGauges.label: Common in choice architecture
teachingGauges.note: Common in menus, pricing, proposals, and interface design.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often obvious once the dominated option is removed from the display.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Constructed preference feels like discovered preference in the moment.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: Very teachable through side-by-side product examples.

practiceLab.intro: Decoy effect shows that preferences are not always waiting inside us fully formed. They can be built through asymmetrical comparison.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A third option is added that is clearly inferior to one main option but not the other.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: The target option suddenly feels like the balanced or obviously superior choice.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Preference shifts because the comparison frame changed, not because the core merits changed.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Strip the decoy out and compare the original options directly before committing.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: Which preference here survives if I only evaluate the original pair?

caseStudies.title: Asymmetric-dominance marketing experiments
caseStudies.summary: Experiments on the decoy effect show that adding a dominated option can reliably shift choice toward the target it makes look stronger by contrast.
caseStudies.whyItFits: The added option changes preference without adding genuine value.

quickCheck: Would I still care this much if the same harm were represented statistically rather than personally?
analogyClaim: This is like lighting a candle for one face and letting a whole dark hillside disappear because it has no single face attached to it.
analogyResponse: Identifiable stories matter, but scale matters too. Moral seriousness should not collapse just because the victims become more numerous and less narratively sharp.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label whenever one story is used to humanize an issue. The distortion begins when the larger invisible scope loses too much weight simply because it is statistical.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when compassion scales badly with the number of affected people and the identifiability of the victims dominates the response.

teachingGauges.label: Common in public response
teachingGauges.note: Highly relevant in media, philanthropy, and policy communication.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often visible when one vivid case overwhelms a far larger statistical harm.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Human attention is naturally drawn toward the identifiable and concrete.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: Needs careful handling so statistical scope is not treated as emotionally optional.

practiceLab.intro: Compassion fade reveals that moral energy is not naturally proportional. Identifiability concentrates feeling while large anonymous harms often flatten out.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: A single vivid victim and a larger anonymous set compete for attention.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: The one face feels more morally real and therefore more urgent than the larger count.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Magnitude loses influence because identifiability and narrative sharpness dominate response.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Keep one concrete story and one scope frame on the page together so the emotional vividness does not erase the larger scale.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: What response would the full scale deserve if I were not allowed to compress it into one emotionally manageable case?

caseStudies.title: Identifiable-victim effect and large-scale helping
caseStudies.summary: Studies on compassion fade show that people can respond more strongly to one identifiable victim than to larger numbers of anonymous victims.
caseStudies.whyItFits: Emotional salience fails to scale with total harm.

quickCheck: What contradiction am I trying to tidy up before I have really examined it?
analogyClaim: This is like hearing two notes clash and reaching for any chord that makes the tension stop, whether or not it is the right music.
analogyResponse: Relief from tension is not the same thing as truth. Contradiction sometimes needs to stay visible long enough for the real revision target to become clear.
misuseWarning: Do not use this label for every uncomfortable disagreement. The issue is a psychologically costly inconsistency that starts motivating reinterpretation, justification, or selective updating.
useLabelWhen: Use this label when people are relieving the discomfort of contradiction by changing stories, standards, or memories rather than confronting the inconsistency directly.

teachingGauges.label: Common in identity conflict
teachingGauges.note: Especially strong where values, self-image, and behavior collide.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to spot from outside
teachingGauges.note: Often hard to see because the repairs feel like ordinary explanation from inside.

teachingGauges.label: Easy to innocently commit
teachingGauges.note: Coherence repair can feel more like sanity than like distortion.

teachingGauges.label: Teaching difficulty
teachingGauges.note: A core concept that becomes much clearer through concrete cases of self-justification.

practiceLab.intro: Cognitive dissonance is the pressure field around inconsistency. Many later biases can be understood as ways of reducing that pressure without openly admitting the revision.

stages.title: Trigger
stages.text: Belief, behavior, identity, or evidence stop fitting together cleanly.

stages.title: Felt certainty
stages.text: The tension feels expensive enough that some fast repair becomes attractive.

stages.title: Distortion
stages.text: Interpretation, memory, or standards shift to restore coherence more cheaply than direct revision would.

stages.title: Reset
stages.text: Name the conflicting commitments explicitly and ask which one really deserves revision rather than patching the contradiction indirectly.

practiceLab.repairQuestion: Which part of this tension would I revise if reducing discomfort were not allowed to make the choice for me?

caseStudies.title: Effort-justification and dissonance reduction research
caseStudies.summary: Classic dissonance research shows that people often revise attitudes or rationales after costly effort or contradictory behavior in order to reduce inner inconsistency.
caseStudies.whyItFits: The repair is aimed at restoring coherence, not merely at discovering a neutral truth.
